section.title: The Priestly Garments
section.sectionRationale: With the structure of the tabernacle described, God now turns to the men who will minister in it: Aaron and his sons are set apart to serve as priests, and their garments, ephod, breastpiece, robe, and turban, are designed for glory and splendor, marking them as mediators who carry Israel before the LORD.
section.thesis: God commands holy garments for Aaron and his sons so that they may serve as priests, carrying the names of Israel's tribes over their hearts and shoulders and bearing the inscription Holy to the LORD on their foreheads.
section.frameNote: Five movements: Aaron and his sons set apart for the priesthood; the ephod bearing the memorial stones; the breastpiece of judgment with the Urim and Thummim; the robe with its bells and pomegranates; and the gold plate, turban, and remaining garments.

units.label: Aaron set apart to serve as priest

media.alt: A priest robed in blue, gold, and white
media.caption: Aaron and his sons are clothed in holy garments to serve the LORD as priests.

verses.text: “Next, have your brother Aaron brought to you from among the Israelites, along with his sons Nadab, Abihu, Eleazar, and Ithamar, to serve Me as priests.
Make holy garments for your brother Aaron, to give him glory and splendor.
You are to instruct all the skilled craftsmen, whom I have filled with a spirit of wisdom, to make garments for Aaron's consecration, so that he may serve Me as priest.

These are the garments that they shall make: a breastpiece, an ephod, a robe, a woven tunic, a turban, and a sash. They are to make these holy garments for your brother Aaron and his sons, so that they may serve Me as priests.
They shall use gold, along with blue, purple, and scarlet yarn, and fine linen.
They are to make the {{ephod|ephod}} of finely spun linen embroidered with gold, and with blue, purple, and scarlet yarn.
It shall have two shoulder pieces attached at two of its corners, so it can be fastened.
And the skillfully woven waistband of the ephod must be of one piece, of the same workmanship, with gold, with blue, purple, and scarlet yarn, and with finely spun linen.
Take two onyx stones and engrave on them the names of the sons of Israel:
six of their names on one stone and the remaining six on the other, in the order of their birth.
Engrave the names of the sons of Israel on the two stones the way a gem cutter engraves a seal. Then mount the stones in gold filigree settings.
Fasten both stones on the shoulder pieces of the ephod as memorial stones for the sons of Israel. Aaron is to bear their names on his two shoulders as a memorial before the LORD.
Fashion gold filigree settings
and two chains of pure gold, made of braided cord work; and attach these chains to the settings.
You are also to make a {{breastpiece|breastpiece}} of judgment with the same workmanship as the ephod. Construct it with gold, with blue, purple, and scarlet yarn, and with finely spun linen.
It must be square when folded over double, a span long and a span wide.
And mount on it a setting of gemstones, four rows of stones: In the first row there shall be a ruby, a topaz, and an emerald;
in the second row a turquoise, a sapphire, and a diamond;
in the third row a jacinth, an agate, and an amethyst;
and in the fourth row a beryl, an onyx, and a jasper. Mount these stones in gold filigree settings.
The twelve stones are to correspond to the names of the sons of Israel, each engraved like a seal with the name of one of the twelve tribes.
For the breastpiece, make braided chains like cords of pure gold.
You are also to make two gold rings and fasten them to the two corners of the breastpiece.
Then fasten the two gold chains to the two gold rings at the corners of the breastpiece,
and fasten the other ends of the two chains to the two filigree settings, attaching them to the shoulder pieces of the ephod at the front.
Make two more gold rings and attach them to the other two corners of the breastpiece, on the inside edge next to the ephod.
Make two additional gold rings and attach them to the bottom of the two shoulder pieces of the ephod, on its front, near its seam just above its woven waistband.
The rings of the breastpiece shall be tied to the rings of the ephod with a cord of blue yarn, so that the breastpiece is above the waistband of the ephod and does not swing out from the ephod.
Whenever Aaron enters the Holy Place, he shall bear the names of the sons of Israel over his heart on the breastpiece of judgment, as a continual reminder before the LORD.

And place the {{urim|Urim and Thummim}} in the breastpiece of judgment, so that they will also be over Aaron's heart whenever he comes before the LORD. Aaron will continually carry the judgment of the sons of Israel over his heart before the LORD.

units.label: The robe, bells, and pomegranates
units.note: so that he will not die

verses.text: You are to make the robe of the ephod entirely of blue cloth,
with an opening at its top in the center. Around the opening shall be a woven collar with an opening like that of a garment, so that it will not tear.
Make pomegranates of blue, purple, and scarlet yarn all the way around the lower hem, with gold bells between them,
alternating the gold bells and pomegranates around the lower hem of the robe.
Aaron must wear the robe whenever he ministers, and its sound will be heard when he enters or exits the sanctuary before the LORD, so that he will not die.

units.label: Holy to the LORD
units.note: the gold plate, turban, and remaining garments

verses.text: You are to make a plate of pure gold and engrave on it as on a seal: {{holy|HOLY TO THE LORD}}.
Fasten to it a blue cord to mount it on the turban; it shall be on the front of the turban.

And it will be worn on Aaron's forehead, so that he may bear the iniquity of the holy things that the sons of Israel consecrate with regard to all their holy gifts. It shall always be on his forehead, so that they may be acceptable before the LORD.
You are to weave the tunic with fine linen, make the turban of fine linen, and fashion an embroidered sash.
Make tunics, sashes, and headbands for Aaron's sons, to give them glory and splendor.
After you put these garments on your brother Aaron and his sons, anoint them, ordain them, and consecrate them so that they may serve Me as priests.
Make linen undergarments to cover their bare flesh, extending from waist to thigh.

Aaron and his sons must wear them whenever they enter the Tent of Meeting or approach the altar to minister in the Holy Place, so that they will not incur guilt and die. This is to be a permanent statute for Aaron and his descendants.

ephod.gloss: A richly woven, apron-like garment worn over the robe, joined at the shoulders and fastened with a waistband. It held the memorial stones bearing Israel's tribal names and anchored the breastpiece to the high priest's body.

breastpiece.gloss: The breastpiece of judgment, a jeweled pouch worn over the heart holding twelve gemstones engraved with the tribal names, and containing the Urim and Thummim used to discern God's will in judgment.

urim.gloss: Urim and Thummim, likely meaning lights and perfections, objects placed in the breastpiece used to discern God's decisions in matters of judgment. Their exact nature is not described, but they functioned as a means of seeking God's verdict.

holy.gloss: Holy to the LORD, an inscription on the gold plate worn on Aaron's forehead. It declared Aaron, and through him the whole nation's worship, set apart exclusively for God, even as he bore the guilt of Israel's imperfect offerings.

basic.context: God now describes the garments Aaron and his sons must wear to serve as priests: an ephod bearing Israel's tribes on the shoulders, a breastpiece bearing them over the heart, a robe with bells so Aaron would not die entering God's presence, and a gold plate reading Holy to the LORD on his forehead.
basic.hermeneutics: These are literal vestments for Israel's actual priesthood under the old covenant, designed with real functions, memorial, mediation, discernment, and holiness, not merely decorative religious costume. Their meaning is drawn out directly by the text itself: memorial stones, a breastpiece of judgment, an inscription of holiness.
basic.originalLanguages: The ephod and choshen, breastpiece, together carried Israel's twelve tribes literally on Aaron's shoulders and over his heart whenever he entered the Holy Place. The urim vetummim within the breastpiece served to discern God's judgment, while the plate reading qodesh la-Yahweh, holy to the LORD, marked Aaron himself as consecrated wholly to God.
basic.typology: Aaron carrying Israel's names on his shoulders and over his heart pictures a mediator who represents the people bodily before God, fulfilled in Christ, who bears His people always before the Father. The plate reading Holy to the LORD anticipates Christ's perfect holiness, which makes even imperfect worship acceptable to God.
basic.christ: Hebrews presents Jesus as the great high priest who always lives to intercede for His people, Hebrews 7:25, carrying them before God far more perfectly than Aaron's engraved stones ever could. Where Aaron bore the guilt of Israel's holy things so they would be accepted, Christ bears His people's sin fully so that they are made acceptable once for all.
basic.god: God cares intensely about how His priests approach Him, prescribing exact garments down to bells on a hem, showing both the seriousness of mediating for a holy God and His gracious provision of a way for that mediation to succeed rather than end in death.
basic.crossReferences: Hebrews 4:14-16 presents Jesus as the great high priest through whom believers approach God's throne with confidence. Hebrews 7:25 says He always lives to intercede for those who come to God through Him. 1 Peter 2:9 calls the whole church a holy priesthood.
basic.oneStory: Aaron was clothed to carry Israel's names before God, bearing their guilt so their worship would be accepted, on pain of death if he entered carelessly. Christ is the true and greater High Priest who carries His people fully and perfectly before the Father, making every believer who trusts Him permanently acceptable before God.

context.literary: This chapter moves from the general purpose of the priestly garments, glory and splendor, glory in service of God rather than mere ceremony, through each specific vestment in the order a priest would put them on, ending with the plain linen undergarments required simply so the priests would not die.
context.historical: The high priest's garments combined the greatest craftsmanship and most costly materials available to Israel, gold, gemstones, and fine woven linen, reflecting both the honor of the priestly office and the seriousness of representing the whole nation before God.
context.canonical: The book of Hebrews draws extensively on this chapter's priestly imagery to explain Christ's superior high priesthood, while Revelation echoes the twelve stones and tribal names in its vision of the new Jerusalem's foundations.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To establish, through detailed and costly vestments, that Aaron's priesthood was a real mediating office carrying Israel bodily before God, requiring holiness on pain of death, and to show God's own provision for that mediation to succeed.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: This passage prescribes the specific garments for Israel's old covenant priesthood; it is not prescriptive for how ministers or clergy should dress today. Its abiding truth is the necessity of a mediator between sinful people and a holy God, fulfilled completely in Christ.

debates.question: What exactly were the Urim and Thummim, and how did they function?

views.label: Sacred lots for yes or no answers
views.case: Many scholars think the Urim and Thummim were something like sacred lots or stones drawn to give a binary answer, similar to other ancient near eastern practices of casting lots to discern a deity's will, as suggested by passages like 1 Samuel 14:41-42.

views.label: An unknown means of divine communication
views.case: Others note Scripture never explains their exact appearance or mechanism, and caution against overconfident reconstruction, since by the time of Ezra their use had apparently ceased, Ezra 2:63, suggesting their function was tied to a specific stage in Israel's history rather than a permanently prescribed method.

debates.bearing: Both views agree the Urim and Thummim served a real, God-given function for discerning His will in Israel's early history, a function later superseded as God's revelation continued through prophets and ultimately through Christ.

study.originalLanguages: The ephod bore two onyx stones on Aaron's shoulders, memorial stones so that Israel's twelve tribes were literally carried before God whenever he ministered. Over his heart hung the choshen, the breastpiece of judgment, holding twelve more gemstones and the urim vetummim, and on his forehead sat a plate declaring qodesh la-Yahweh, holy to the LORD, over the entire nation's worship.

typology.type: Aaron bearing Israel's tribes on his shoulders and over his heart
typology.fulfillment: Christ, the great High Priest who continually intercedes for His people before the Father, Hebrews 7:25.

typology.type: The plate reading Holy to the LORD, bearing the guilt of Israel's imperfect offerings
typology.fulfillment: Christ's perfect holiness, which makes believers and their worship fully acceptable to God, Hebrews 10:10.

study.christ: Hebrews describes Jesus as a high priest who has passed through the heavens, able to sympathize with weaknesses yet without sin, and who always lives to intercede for those who draw near to God through Him, Hebrews 4:14-15, 7:25. Where Aaron's mediation depended on garments, blood, and careful ritual to avoid death, Christ's own perfect life and sacrifice permanently secure believers' access to God.
study.god: God prescribes exact, costly garments for approaching Him, showing that mediating for a holy God is serious enough to require holiness on pain of death, verse 35 and verse 43. Yet He also provides the very garments and inscription, Holy to the LORD, that make that mediation succeed, revealing His desire that His people's imperfect worship still be accepted.

crossReferences.note: Presents Jesus as the great high priest through whom believers can approach God's throne of grace with confidence.
Christ always lives to intercede for those who come to God through Him.
Describes the whole church as a chosen and holy priesthood in Christ.

study.oneStory: Aaron was clothed with garments that literally carried Israel's tribes before God, on pain of death if he entered the sanctuary carelessly, and a gold plate reading Holy to the LORD covered the guilt of Israel's imperfect worship. That priesthood finds its fulfillment in Jesus Christ, the perfectly holy High Priest who carries His people before the Father forever and makes their worship fully acceptable.

quiz.q: What was the stated purpose of Aaron's holy garments, according to verse 2?

options.text: To give him glory and splendor.
options.note: Verse 2 states this exact purpose for the garments.

options.text: To disguise him from the rest of Israel.
options.note: The garments were meant to display honor and holiness openly, not to conceal or disguise Aaron.

options.text: Purely for practical warmth in the wilderness.
options.note: No such practical purpose is mentioned; the stated reason is glory and splendor for his priestly office.

options.text: No purpose is given for the garments.
options.note: Verse 2 explicitly states the purpose: to give Aaron glory and splendor.

quiz.q: What was engraved on the two onyx stones mounted on the ephod's shoulder pieces?

options.text: The names of the sons of Israel, six on each stone.
options.note: Verses 9 through 10 describe this exact engraving.

options.text: The Ten Commandments in full.
options.note: The commandments were written on stone tablets kept in the ark, not engraved on the shoulder stones.

options.text: The name of Moses alone.
options.note: The stones bore the names of all twelve tribes, not a single individual's name.

options.text: The stones were left unengraved.
options.note: Verses 9 through 11 explicitly describe the engraving of Israel's tribal names.

quiz.q: What was placed inside the breastpiece of judgment, according to verse 30?

options.text: The Urim and Thummim.
options.note: Verse 30 specifies these were placed in the breastpiece over Aaron's heart.

options.text: A vial of anointing oil.
options.note: Anointing oil is described separately in chapter 30; the breastpiece specifically held the Urim and Thummim.

options.text: A copy of the covenant.
options.note: The covenant tablets, the Testimony, were placed in the ark, not the breastpiece.

options.text: The breastpiece was left empty.
options.note: Verse 30 explicitly commands the Urim and Thummim be placed within it.

quiz.q: Why did the robe of the ephod have gold bells and pomegranates around its hem?

options.text: So the sound would be heard when Aaron entered or exited the sanctuary, so that he would not die.
options.note: Verse 35 gives this exact, sober reason for the bells.

options.text: Purely for decorative musical effect during festivals.
options.note: The text gives a specific protective purpose tied to entering the sanctuary, not merely festive decoration.

options.text: To identify Aaron's rank compared to his sons.
options.note: The bells' stated purpose concerns entering the sanctuary safely, not signaling rank among priests.

options.text: No reason is given for the bells.
options.note: Verse 35 explicitly explains their purpose regarding entering and exiting the sanctuary.

quiz.q: What was inscribed on the gold plate worn on Aaron's forehead?

options.text: HOLY TO THE LORD.
options.note: Verse 36 gives this exact inscription.

options.text: The name of each tribe of Israel.
options.note: Tribal names were engraved on the shoulder stones and breastpiece gems, not on the forehead plate.

options.text: The Ten Commandments.
options.note: The commandments were written on the stone tablets in the ark, not on the forehead plate.

options.text: The plate bore no inscription.
options.note: Verse 36 explicitly gives the inscription: Holy to the LORD.

questions: Aaron carried Israel's names on his shoulders and over his heart every time he entered God's presence. How does it change your prayer life to know Christ carries your name before the Father in exactly that way, but perfectly?

The garments were made 'for glory and splendor,' yet their deepest purpose was to make Aaron's mediation possible at all. Where do you find yourself valuing the outward appearance of faith more than what it actually accomplishes before God?

The bells on Aaron's robe warned that carelessly entering God's presence could mean death. How does remembering the seriousness of approaching a holy God shape the reverence, or the casualness, of your own approach to Him?

The plate on Aaron's forehead let Israel's imperfect worship be counted holy before God. Where are you currently trying to make your own worship or service acceptable through effort, rather than resting in Christ's holiness on your behalf?

The Urim and Thummim gave Israel a way to seek God's judgment in specific matters. How do you currently seek God's guidance for decisions, and where might you need to trust Him more and control the outcome less?

translationNotes.note: The exact appearance and mechanism of the Urim and Thummim are not described anywhere in Scripture; they appear to have functioned as a means of discerning God's judgment, and their use had apparently ceased by the postexilic period, Ezra 2:63.

Bear the iniquity of the holy things describes Aaron's role in absorbing whatever ritual imperfection accompanied Israel's offerings, so that their gifts would still be accepted before the LORD, a striking anticipation of substitutionary mediation.

So that they will not incur guilt and die is repeated from verse 35 regarding the bells, underscoring that careless or improper approach to a holy God carried real, fatal consequences under the old covenant.
